paragraphs.body: Fast Food in China Thanks to the economic reforms and social changes since the late 1970s, not only has China's overall market economy developed tremendously, but China's fast food industry has also reached its peak in the past three decades. After the Cultural Revolution(1966-1976) and the reforms that required the elimination of the "Four Olds" and the government's guidance of China towards a capitalist market in 1979, mainland China became a fertile group for Western culture. [1]Similarly, these factors have promoted and influenced the pattern of modern fast food in China.

International Fast Food Brands Western fast food entered China in the 1980s, with KFC opening its first store in Beijing in 1987, followed by McDonald's in 1990. Subsequently, other international brands including Subway, Domino's Pizza, and Burger King joined the market, continuously expanding the industry. As of April 2023, KFC has 9,650 stores in China, McDonald's has 5,746 stores, Burger King has 1,494 stores, and Subway has 718 stores. These international chains dominate the market, with McDonald's, KFC, Burger King, and Wallace holding over 90% market share in 2022. For Chinese people growing up in the 1990s, Western fast food represented a novel experience.[2]

Domestic Fast Food Innovation Tastien(塔斯汀) was founded in 2012 and has rapidly expanded to more than 7,000 stores nationwide in recent years. Tastien is a domestic fast food brand known for its handmade, freshly baked Chinese hamburgers. It combines Western burgers with traditional Chinese flavors, offering dishes such as Peking duck, garlic pork, and Mapo Tofu. Beijing-based Nan Cheng Xiang(南城香) achieved RMB 1.5 billion in revenue last year through a community business model and an all-day service model. Micun BanFan(米村拌饭) in Yanji, Jilin Province, has grown to 1,000 stores and is known as the "McDonald's of Northeast China." These domestic chains have succeeded in the fast food market through competitive pricing, which is typically 30-40% lower than international chains.[3]

paragraphs.body: Digital Integration and Modern Services Research shows that China now has 753 million mobile Internet users, of which 506 million shop through mobile Internet. Newly emerging food delivery platforms and the large number of digital coupons provided by offline merchants have become the key to attracting customers to use mobile ordering platforms, while website quality and brand equity have a great impact on consumers' willingness to use fast food mobile commerce. In online ordering preferences, burgers rank third among the top 10 food and beverage categories, after milk tea and rice dishes.[4]

annotations.comment: I would add hyperlinks to other Wikipedia articles.

paragraphs.body: The Develop Market China's fast food market shows distinct consumer demographics and changing preferences. Young adults aged 25-34 form the largest group, representing 44% of fast food consumers.[5] This trend reflects China's fast-paced urban lifestyle and limited lunch breaks for office workers. With many companies lacking canteens, employees rely on external dining options. Over half of Chinese consumers visit Western fast food restaurants, while 40.4% prefer fusion-style fast food, showing a growing demand for mixed cuisine.[6]

The industry has gained new trends in health consciousness. Fast food chains are responding by offering more vegetarian and vegan options.[7] KFC China launched its first plant-based chicken nuggets in 2020, receiving a positive consumer response.[8]
Meanwhile, domestic brand Bingz(西少爷肉夹馍) is expanding internationally, operating in Canada and planning to enter the U.S. market.[9]

What started as a simple Thursday discount at KFC China in 2018 transformed into an internet sensation by 2021, all thanks to five simple words: "疯狂星期四V我50元" (Crazy Thursday, send me 50 yuan). This catchphrase didn't just promote a deal – it spawned an entire internet culture. The campaign's genius emerged when KFC began leveraging celebrity endorsements and encouraging user-generated memes. Soon, Thursdays became more than just a day for discounted fried chicken; they became a weekly social media event where millions of Chinese netizens shared creative content around the "V我50" theme.[10]
